lines.text: Section six. Designer cells — moving beyond copying nature.
Synthesising a Mycoplasma genome that matches a natural genome is impressive, but it is also conservative.
The much more interesting prospect is producing cells with genomes that have never existed in nature — cells designed from the bottom up to do specific things.
The early target for such a designer cell is the minimal Mycoplasma we mentioned earlier: M. laboratorium, with as few genes as possible while remaining viable.
Building M. laboratorium would have several advantages.

Its reduced gene count would make it much simpler to model mathematically — the cellular dynamics could in principle be simulated with realistic accuracy, because there would be fewer interacting components.

Its streamlined nature would make it more efficient — less metabolic overhead spent on running pathways that the engineered application does not need, more resources available for the engineered behaviour itself.

Because the genome was built from defined parts, it could be modified easily: gene additions, deletions, swaps would all be straightforward, because the genome structure is documented down to the base pair.
Beyond M. laboratorium, the textbook points at the broader design space.
Synthesis lets you not just copy genomes; it lets you recode them.

One striking example, which the textbook explicitly cites, is the Genomically Recoded Organism — a recoded E. coli strain in which every TAG stop codon in the genome was replaced with TAA, freeing up the TAG codon to be reassigned to non-standard amino acids.
With the TAG codon reassigned, researchers can introduce non-natural amino acids into proteins under genetic control — building proteins from 21 amino acids instead of the natural 20.

This opens up enormous new chemistry: enzymes with non-natural side chains, proteins with site-specific reactive handles for bioconjugation, fluorescent amino acids built directly into the polypeptide.
Recoding the genome by hand to enable this kind of work would have been essentially impossible before genome synthesis; with synthesis, it became tractable.

An even more ambitious project is Sc2.0 — Synthetic Yeast 2.0 — which aims to construct a recoded synthetic version of the entire 11 million base pair S. cerevisiae genome, spread across 16 chromosomes, with many sequence changes designed to benefit research and biotechnology.
By the time of this revised edition, one of the 16 chromosomes had been completed, much of the work performed by undergraduate students as part of the project's pedagogical mission.
If Sc2.0 succeeds, we will have an entire eukaryotic organism whose genome was written, top to bottom, by humans — a milestone arguably as significant as the original M. mycoides synthesis.
Why else might you want a designer cell?
Beyond gene minimisation and codon recoding, the authors list two important categories.
First, evolutionary stability.
Many synthetic biology applications — especially biosensors and long-deployed engineered systems — need the genetic structure of the cell to remain stable over many generations.
Natural cells, however, mutate.

And evolution selects against engineered burdens: an engineered cell carrying around an extra pathway it does not need for survival will, over many generations, lose that pathway as variants that have lost it grow faster.
Engineering chassis cells with reduced recombination, with deliberate DNA repair, with removed transposons and removed mutation hotspots gives you a much more stable platform.
Such a cell would resist evolution, holding its engineered design for longer.
Second, the opposite — engineered evolution.
Sometimes you want a cell to evolve, but quickly and in controlled directions.

Directed evolution is already a major technology in drug discovery and metabolic engineering — generating diversity in a gene of interest, applying selection, watching for variants with improved properties.
Cells engineered for fast evolution — with abundant recombination hotspots and reduced DNA repair — could accelerate directed evolution workflows substantially.
Each engineering goal points toward a different designer cell.
The single shared idea is that the chassis is no longer a fixed natural organism whose properties we must accept; it becomes a design surface.
And the way the field is heading, the chassis itself becomes part of the engineering decision.
